title: Kanazawa's Gardens & Nature
standfirst: Kenroku-en alone justifies the trip — one of Japan's three great gardens, 200 years in the making. Then there are the two others you'll want to visit on the same day.
intro_html: Kenroku-en was constructed in stages by the Maeda clan — the ruling lords of the Kaga domain — over a period of roughly two centuries, beginning in the early 1600s and reaching its completed form in the mid-1800s. The name itself is a clue to the garden's ambition: kenroku means "combining six attributes," referring to the six qualities held in classical Chinese landscape theory to be impossible to achieve all at once in a single garden — spaciousness, seclusion, artifice, antiquity, watercourses, and panoramic views. Kenroku-en achieves all six. That is the garden's defining boast, and after walking it, you will find the boast is not empty.

attractions.name: Kenroku-en Garden
attractions.description: One of Japan's three most beautiful landscape gardens. Developed over 200 years by the Maeda clan, it incorporates the six attributes of a perfect garden: spaciousness, seclusion, artifice, antiquity, water-courses, and panoramic views. Famous for the Kotojitoro lantern and its stunning seasonal beauty.

attractions.name: Gyokusen-inmaru Garden
attractions.description: A small jewel-garden built in the 17th century, tucked inside the Kanazawa Castle grounds. More intimate than Kenroku-en — white castle walls reflected in still water, stone bridges, a traditional tea house. Often overlooked by visitors who only walk the castle exterior.

attractions.name: Nomura Garden
attractions.description: Inside the Nomura Samurai House in Nagamachi — tiny but exquisite. A miniature garden with a stream, stone lantern, koi, and a maple tree that frames the view perfectly. Best seen from the upper-floor tatami room. One of the most quietly beautiful spots in Kanazawa.

attractions.name: Kenzan Kiln Garden
attractions.description: The historic garden of Kenzan — a celebrated Kanazawa ceramic artist whose distinctive style influenced Japanese pottery for centuries. The kiln site has been preserved as a small free garden. Less visited, more meditative, and a good excuse to learn about the city's pottery heritage.

getting_there.text: The most convenient option. The Kanazawa Loop Bus runs two routes (left loop and right loop) from Kanazawa Station East Exit. The Kenroku-en/Kanazawa Castle stop is on both routes — approximately 15 minutes, ¥200 per ride. A 1-day pass (¥600) covers unlimited loop bus rides and is good value if you're visiting multiple sights.
Approximately 25–30 minutes along a straightforward route through the city centre. Flat terrain for most of the walk. Good option on a clear morning — you pass through the old merchant district.
Around ¥1,200–1,500 from the station. Available immediately outside the East Exit. Use for late arrivals or when carrying luggage.

Paid parking is available near the north and south entrances. Not recommended on spring or autumn weekends — the roads around the garden are extremely congested. The Loop Bus is a far better choice on peak days.

faqs.question: What are the opening hours for Kenroku-en?
faqs.answer: Kenroku-en is open 7am–6pm from March through October, and 8am–5pm from November through February. Night illuminations during cherry blossom and autumn foliage season extend the evening hours — check the official schedule for the current year. The garden sometimes opens before the ticketed hours; arriving at dawn during cherry season is highly recommended even if you have to wait briefly at the gate.

faqs.question: How much does Kenroku-en cost and is it worth it?
faqs.answer: Adult admission is ¥320 — genuinely one of the best-value admission charges in Japan. A combination ticket with Kanazawa Castle's Hishi Yagura tower costs ¥640. Children under 18 enter free. Gyokusen-inmaru Garden (inside the castle grounds) costs an additional ¥100. Yes: absolutely worth it. It is one of the finest gardens in the world by any measure.

faqs.question: How long should I spend at Kenroku-en?
faqs.answer: Allow 1.5 to 2 hours to walk the full garden at a comfortable pace, including time at the Kotoji lantern, the upper plateau, the Seisonkaku Villa gate area, and the iris garden. An hour is enough for a quick visit but you will feel rushed. If you are visiting in cherry or autumn season and want to photograph properly, 2.5–3 hours is more realistic.

faqs.question: Can I visit Kenroku-en and the castle in the same day?
faqs.answer: Yes — they are directly adjacent, connected by the Kenroku-en gate and a short path. A typical morning visits Kenroku-en first (7–9am to avoid crowds), then moves to the castle park, then Gyokusen-inmaru Garden, then walks the Nagamachi samurai quarter in the afternoon. All of this is walkable without any transport.

faqs.question: Is Kenroku-en crowded?
faqs.answer: On cherry blossom and autumn foliage weekends, Kenroku-en can be very crowded — particularly between 9am and 3pm. The solution is simple: arrive when the gate opens (7am in summer season). The early-morning light is also the best for photography. Weekday visits at any time of year are significantly quieter.
